title: Sleep Disruptions and Information Processing in Financial Markets
abstract: Despite evidence of the importance of sleep for cognitive performance, prior research finds limited effects of sleep disruptions on financial markets. This is puzzling because financial decisions rely on higher-order cognitive processes that are typically affected by sleep. We reconcile this dissonance by examining forecasts of corporate earnings by nonprofessional and professional forecasters. We find that nonprofessional forecasters exhibit a significant decline in their forecast accuracy following spring daylight saving time changes relative to professional forecasters. Using a separate sample of professional equity analysts’ forecasts, we continue to find that the information processing of analysts with less expertise is disproportionately negatively affected following sleep disruptions. Placebo tests provide support for sleep disruptions as the main driver of these effects. Overall, our evidence that information processing by less experienced and skilled market participants is particularly vulnerable to sleep disruptions provides one compelling reason why aggregate financial markets seem to be immune to sleep effects even though individual participants are not.

author.William Bazley: School of Business, University of Kansas, Lawrence, Kansas 66045
author.Carina Cuculiza: Spears School of Business, Oklahoma State University, Stillwater, Oklahoma 74078
author.Kevin Pisciotta: School of Business, University of Kansas, Lawrence, Kansas 66045

article.1. Introduction: Sleep is vital to cognitive performance. Insufficient sleep impairs memory, leads to rigid thinking, and reduces information processing ability, particularly in the context of complex decisions.1Consistent with reduced cognitive performance,Williamson and Feyer (2000)find that after 17–19 hours without sleep, individuals perform similarly or worse on laboratory tests than if they had a blood alcohol content (BAC) of 0.05%; after longer periods, individuals perform as if they had a BAC above the legal driving limit.2Even minor sleep disruptions, such as those arising from the spring shift to daylight saving time (DST), reduce performance in complex tasks like driving (Monk 1980,Hicks et al. 1983,Coren 1996,Varughese and Allen 2001,Smith 2016). Given that sleep disruptions impede higher-order cognitive processes, and that higher-order cognitive processes are vital for information processing in financial markets, it is reasonable to expect that sleep disruptions would affect information processing and decision making in financial markets. Understanding how sleep affects information processing is important because information processing—that is, the cognitive mechanism by which market participants consume, organize, and interpret financial information—affects capital allocation decisions (e.g.,Derrien and Kecskes 2013,Mola et al. 2013,Li 2020). Importantly though, the existing evidence on the effect of sleep on information processing in financial markets is inconclusive.Kamstra et al. (2000)provide evidence that sleep disruptions resulting from daylight saving time clock shifts are associated with returns that are two to five times more negative than non-daylight-saving-time periods (a loss of about $31B per day on the NYSE, NASDAQ, and AMEX exchanges). However,Pinegar (2002),Lamb et al. (2004), andBerument et al. (2010)suggest that DST clock shifts do not substantially impact aggregate market returns or volatility. It remains a puzzle then why sleep disruptions have little effect on financial market outcomes, particularly because existing evidence connects other environmental and physiological factors—such as weather-induced changes in mood—to aggregate market movements.3 In this paper, we approach the question of how sleep disruptions affect information processing in financial markets from a new perspective: we examine the effects of sleep disruptions on the information processing of individual market participants by focusing on corporate earnings forecasts by professional and novice forecasters. Specifically, we analyze earnings-per-share (EPS) forecasts submitted to data provider Estimize by both buy-side/sell-side professional forecasters and nonprofessional forecasters.4This setting provides a number of advantages for studying the effect of sleep disruptions on information processing in financial markets. First, information processing is fundamental to an analyst’s ability to produce accurate forecasts of future earnings (Hirshleifer et al. 2019). Second, we are able to unambiguously assess the quality of an analyst’s information processing ability using realized earnings. Third, we can observe the same analyst engaging in the same task on days with sleep disruptions and days without. Fourth, we can benchmark forecasts issued by analysts likely exposed to sleep disruptions to forecasts issued by peer analysts less likely exposed to sleep disruptions but forecasting the same firm around the same time. Lastly, we can assess the effects of sleep disruptions as a function of task expertise, which allows us to test key predictions from prior studies on the asymmetric effects of cognitive limitations on individuals’ information processing. Thus, we have a clear measure of information processing quality, several methods to estimate plausible counterfactuals, and a measure of the expertise of market participants. None of these features are available when studying aggregate effects at the stock market level. We begin our analysis by examining individual forecasters’ earnings forecast accuracy around the beginning of DST each year, relative to the forecast accuracy of other forecasters forecasting the same firms and earnings dates during the surrounding days. More specifically, we classify the two trading days following spring DST Sunday as our treatment period and compare forecasters’ accuracy during that period with forecasters’ accuracy during the 30 calendar days before and after the beginning and end of the treatment period, respectively. Our focus on a treatment window that extends three days after the clock shift is motivated by prior research indicating that one hour of sleep loss takes three to four days to recover from (Harrison 2013,Kitamura et al. 2016). In these analyses, we find no significant change in forecast accuracy during the period immediately following spring DST. Sleep disruptions, however, do not likely affect the information processing ability of all market participants equally. Sleep research finds that the cognitive abilities of older and more experienced individuals are less susceptible to sleep disruptions than those of younger and less experienced individuals (Schwarz et al. 2019). Similarly,Lee et al. (2021)find that more experienced flight crews are less affected by sleep disruptions than younger flight crews. Furthermore, existing literature highlights that learning from prior experience can reduce the cognitive demands for a particular task and support performance (Wright 1936,Yelle 1979,Schneider and Fisk 1982,Mikhail et al. 1997,Fairclough et al. 2005). As a result, “expert” forecasters with more experience likely drain fewer critical thinking resources when processing financial information compared with other forecasters. We thus predict that sleep disruptions disproportionately affect the information processing of forecasters with less expertise. Leveraging the unique ability to separate forecasts issued by novices from those issued by professionals in our data from Estimize, we find empirical support for this prediction. We show that the gap in accuracy between forecasts issued by professional and nonprofessional forecasters significantly widens following spring DST: nonprofessional forecasters become an additional 18.3% less accurate compared with professional forecasters during two days following spring DST. Our inclusion of firm-earnings period fixed effects, in addition to day-of-the-week and forecaster fixed effects and a bevy of forecaster and forecast control variables interacted with our measure of expertise, makes it unlikely that the relative decline in forecast accuracy among novice forecasters is due to confounding effects. Further, we show that forecasters issuing forecasts following spring DST are observably similar to forecasters issuing forecasts during surrounding days, and the relative distribution of professional and nonprofessional forecasts during each period is similar. This evidence suggests that our results are not driven by a separating equilibrium whereby only low-quality forecasters issue forecasts after daylight saving time clock shifts, so as to be able to blame their performance on the time change. To provide further support for the notion that reductions in forecast accuracy following spring DST are due to sleep disruptions and not spurious correlation, we conduct several additional tests. First, we perform a parallel trends analysis using the weeks before and after spring DST. We find no significant differences in the trends of relative forecast accuracy between nonprofessional and professional forecasters during the weeks surrounding spring DST. Second, we conduct placebo tests using the week before spring DST changes. We find no differences in relative forecast accuracy during the placebo window. Third, we examine relative analyst forecast accuracy surrounding fall DST, when an extra hour is afforded. Consistent with prior evidence documenting that fall DST does not meaningfully affect individuals’ quantity or quality of sleep and that as a result fall DST has little effect on performance outcomes (see, e.g.,Barnes and Wagner 2009,Smith 2016), we find no evidence that the relative accuracy of nonprofessional forecasters changes following fall DST. Last, we perform two out-of-sample tests using a sample of professional sell-side analyst forecasts from I/B/E/S. In the first out-of-sample test, we replicate both of our Estimize-sample analyses—pooled tests and cross-sectional tests based on forecaster expertise—using a sample covering the same time period from I/B/E/S. In these analyses, we continue to find that (i) average forecast accuracy does not change during the days immediately following spring DST and (ii) less experienced professional analysts become relatively less accurate compared with their more experienced counterparts. These results are consistent with our earlier evidence and support the notion that sleep disruptions have asymmetric effects on the information processing of financial market participants, which widen the gap in information processing quality between skilled and unskilled participants. In the second out-of-sample test, to examine whether our estimates are driven purely by the timing of the spring DST clock shifts in the United States, we use international data from I/B/E/S to study the effect of spring DST on analysts exposed to DST policies differing from those of the United States. We find a significant relative decline in forecast accuracy among low-expertise analysts in Brazil, which is the most populated I/B/E/S sample among Southern Hemisphere countries beginning their DST during the fall in the United States. We also find no significant change in relative accuracy around the U.S. spring DST period among analysts in Japan or South Korea, the two most populated I/B/E/S samples among eastern Asian countries that do not shift their clocks. These findings suggest our results are likely due to sleep disruptions, generalize to other financial markets, and are not specific to the timing of spring DST in the United States. This paper contributes broadly to research examining the effects of sleep disruptions on cognitive performance. Prior literature finds robust evidence that sleep disruptions negatively affect cognition and motor skills in tasks like operating motor vehicles, physical occupations, athletic performance, and physiological performance (see, e.g.,Mah et al. 2011,Kitamura et al. 2016,Smith 2016). However, existing literature finds mixed evidence on the effects of sleep disruptions on information processing in financial markets (Kamstra et al. 2000,Pinegar 2002,Lamb et al. 2004,Kleppe et al. 2024). We add new insights to this literature by exploiting the analyst setting, where we can observe high-frequency outcomes for individual financial market participants and can uniquely test predictions about how the effects of sleep disruptions vary with participants’ levels of expertise. We show that sleep disruptions reduce information processing quality, but only among intermediaries with less domain-specific expertise. This finding is important for investors not only because it highlights an asymmetry in how sleep disruptions affect information provided by intermediaries, but also because it suggests that investors’ own abilities to process information are potentially more affected by sleep disruptions when they have less expertise. We also contribute to the literature on the effects of cognitive limitations in capital markets.Nekrasov et al. (2023)review this literature and note that prior research provides evidence that more sophisticated investors are less affected by cognitive limitations than retail investors (e.g.,Bartov et al. 2000). Using underexplored data providing information on analysts’ professional backgrounds, we find that shocks to cognitive limitations from spring DST clock shifts disproportionately negatively affect the forecast accuracy of novice forecasters. We further contribute to literature focused on identifying factors that affect analysts’ abilities to process information. Prior research identifies a number of such factors, including analyst characteristics and resources (e.g.,Clement 1999,Jacob et al. 2008,Kumar 2010,Bradley et al. 2017), information channels (e.g.,Malloy 2005,Cohen et al. 2010,Green et al. 2014,Soltes 2014), environmental factors such as pollution (e.g.,Dong et al. 2019,Li et al. 2020), and incentives (e.g.,Michaely and Womack 1999,Hong et al. 2000,Hong and Kubik 2003,Ljungqvist et al. 2006,Harford et al. 2019,Lourie 2019). Additionally, several recent papers examine cognitive bias and limitations to information processing capacity (e.g.,Hirshleifer et al. 2019,Driskill et al. 2020,Pisciotta 2023). Our paper builds on these studies by providing evidence that sleep disruptions reduce forecast quality, but only among more novice forecasters, which represents novel evidence that more experienced analysts better absorb the influence of an impediment to human processing capabilities than their less experienced peers. In a related paper,Jannati (2024)study the effect of restfulness on analyst forecast accuracy. Using six national holidays in the United States as a shock to restfulness, they provide evidence consistent with rest improving performance. In contrast to their paper, (i) we focus on shocks to sleep disruptions, (ii) we believe that our use of a well-supported shock to sleep disruptions and our identification strategy that holds constant individual firm-earnings periods better enables us to identify the effect of sleep disruptions, (iii) we focus on the differential effects of sleep disruptions on the information processing of market participants with differing levels of expertise, and (iv) we exploit cross-country data to support our main analysis and the external validity of our results. Last, our findings have broad implications for both industry professionals and policymakers. A recent discussion among industry professionals involves junior analyst burnout. In March of 2021,Bloombergreported that a group of junior analysts at Goldman Sachs released a report detailing 105-hour workweeks and consistent 9 a.m.–5 a.m. schedules due to hectic deal flow that left many analysts sleep deprived and “in a really dark place” (Levine 2021). Our evidence suggests that less experienced analysts are more prone to making information processing mistakes when they are sleep deprived than their more experienced colleagues. In addition, a recurring discussion among policymakers involves the push to do away with clock shifts in the United States. Our evidence suggests another cost of maintaining the current system is that spring clock shifts disrupt the relative information processing abilities of less experienced individuals in ways that are unique relative to countries that do not share the United States’ DST system.
article.2. Hypothesis Development: Small disruptions in sleep can meaningfully affect cognitive, physical, and physiological performance. For instance,Medic et al. (2017)find that short-term disruptions in sleep negatively affect cognition, memory, and performance among healthy adults, andMah et al. (2011)find that sleep disruptions negatively affect physical performance among college athletes. Similarly, several studies provide evidence that sleep disruptions increase the incidence of workplace and auto accidents (Monk 1980,Hicks et al. 1983,Coren 1996,Varughese and Allen 2001,Smith 2016). In a review of the sleep research literature,Pilcher and Huffcutt (1996)find that sleep disruptions strongly impair human functioning, including cognitive and motor performance. An equally supported fact in the literature is that the one-hour clock shift at the beginning of daylight saving time in the spring of each year in the United States meaningfully affects sleep quality. For example,Kantermann et al. (2007)show that humans’ circadian rhythm systems have difficulty adjusting to spring DST because of disruptions in melatonin production and peripheral gene expression. Additionally,Barnes and Wagner (2009)show that spring DST reduces worker sleep on average by 40 minutes, andHarrison (2013, p. 285)concludes that “adjustment to daylight saving time is neither immediate nor without consequence” (abstract). Thus, many of the documented effects of sleep disruptions that researchers show in laboratory experiments occur following spring DST clock shifts because the one-hour clock shift meaningfully disrupts people’s sleep. For example,Barnes and Wagner (2009)provide evidence that the reduction in sleep following spring DST is the likely cause of accompanying increases in workplace injuries.5 Despite the aforementioned evidence, few studies directly examine the effects of sleep disruptions on information processing in financial markets. Further, the existing evidence on the consequences of sleep disruptions in financial markets is mixed.Kamstra et al. (2000)find that returns are systemically more negative over DST clock change weekends than over other weekends, consistent with the notion that sleep disruptions impact aggregate decision making in financial markets. However,Lamb et al. (2004)find that in roughly half of theKamstra et al. (2000)sample of DST clock changes, returns are higher than typical weekend returns, and the authors provide evidence that returns during spring DST clock changes do not on average differ from normal weekend returns.Pinegar (2002)andGregory-Allen et al. (2010)reach similar conclusions, providing evidence that movements in aggregate market returns or volatility do not support the conclusion that there is a daylight-saving-time effect in financial markets. A challenge with studying the effects of sleep disruptions on information processing in financial markets is that there are limited data on the decision making by individual market participants. Thus, researchers traditionally try to infer the effects of sleep disruptions using variation in aggregate financial market outcomes (e.g., market-wide returns, volatility, etc.). However, aggregate market outcomes are the result of equilibrium behavior of affected participants (investors subject to sleep disruptions) and unaffected participants (investors less subject to sleep disruptions). Unaffected participants have incentives to exploit the decisions of affected participants. Aggregate market outcomes also preclude direct measures of information processing quality—for example, there is no agreed-upon measure of stock return accuracy. Further, aggregate market outcomes surrounding DST clock shifts (such as Friday-to-Monday returns) have a low-frequency problem that confounds post-DST changes in behavior with pre-DST changes. Last, it is empirically challenging to separate microlevel effects (i.e., at the individual level) from aggregate outcomes. We attempt to overcome these limitations by focusing on the earnings forecasts of individual market participants. Earnings forecasts offer a way to (i) directly measure the quality of information processing at the individual level via the accuracy of each participant’s forecasts and (ii) exploit high-frequency decision outcomes to compare the information processing quality of affected and unaffected participants performing the same task under different sleep circumstances. We expect that sleep disruptions affect participants’ forecast accuracy for the following reasons.6First, forecasting future cash flows is a challenging task that drains System 2 critical thinking resources.Hirshleifer et al. (2019)show that the more forecasts an analyst issues earlier in the day, and thus the more System 2 thinking resources the analyst drains, the less accurate the analyst’s subsequent forecasts are. Their evidence supports a broader literature on bounded rationality and limited cognitive capacity documenting that factors which drain mental resources impair subsequent information processing ability (e.g.,Peng 2005,Gabaix et al. 2006,Levav et al. 2010). Second, as we suggest above and asHarrison and Horne (1999)document, sleep disruptions reduce cognitive performance and the ability to process information when making complex decisions. Consequently, we expect sleep disruptions to negatively affect analysts’ information processing abilities and reduce their forecast accuracy. Importantly, sleep disruptions do not likely affect the information processing ability of all market participants equally. More broadly, a body of sleep research finds that sleep disruptions have weaker effects on older and more experienced individuals. For example,Schwarz et al. (2019)show that cognitive attenuation following sleep deprivation is less pronounced among older people compared with younger people. Likewise,Lee et al. (2021)find that more experienced flight crews are less affected by sleep disruptions than younger flight crews. Prior studies also show that learning from prior experience can reduce the cognitive demands for a particular task and improve performance (Wright 1936,Yelle 1979,Schneider and Fisk 1982,Mikhail et al. 1997,Fairclough et al. 2005). Thus, more experience in a decision task might moderate the decline in information processing quality that occurs when individuals are sleep deprived. More specific to financial market participants, several prior papers provide evidence that expertise reduces the effect of market participants’ cognitive constraints. For example,Bartov et al. (2000)find that the effects of limited attention are more pronounced for less sophisticated investors compared with institutional investors,Kaustia et al. (2008)find that investors’ “anchoring” behavioral bias is more pronounced for novice investors compared with professional investors, andPisciotta (2023)shows that more experienced analysts better manage increases in their workload than less experienced analysts. One intuitive way in which expertise may reduce analysts’ susceptibility to sleep disruptions is that analysts with more expertise typically have greater industry knowledge and domain-specific experience, as well as deeper connections to insiders, all of which can dampen the impact of sleep disruptions on information processing quality. Our central hypothesis is thus that forecasters produce less accurate forecasts on days following sleep disruptions than on other days but that this effect is concentrated among forecasters with less domain-specific expertise. Our focus on individual equity market forecasters, as opposed to aggregate market outcomes, uniquely enables us to examine whether the effects of sleep disruptions vary by domain-specific expertise. The prior evidence mentioned above also motivates a second hypothesis, specifically related to professional analysts. Although we expect the information processing of analysts with less expertise to be more sensitive to sleep disruptions, the effect of analyst expertise may be moderated by the quality of an analyst’s resources, which can support the analyst’s performance when it might otherwise suffer. For example, professional analysts are typically supported by teams of junior analysts (Fang and Hope 2021), extensive data and software resources, and connections with industry insiders and talented colleagues, all of which analysts can lean on when they are sleep deprived. Because these resources tend to be increasing in the reputation of the analyst’s brokerage, we predict that the effect of low analyst expertise on the sensitivity to sleep disruptions is mitigated by the size and reputation of the analyst’s employer.
article.3. Data: Our primary sample of EPS forecasts comes from Estimize. Estimize crowdsources quarterly earnings forecasts from novice and professional forecasters via an online platform. Since 2011, Estimize has grown to incorporate forecasts that span more than 3,000 stocks from over 100,000 forecasters, including buy- and sell-side analysts, as well as novice forecasters with a wide range of professional backgrounds (e.g., academics, students, industrial firm employees, etc.). Table IA1 in the internet appendix reports the distribution of professions among novice forecasters. Approximately 30% of novice forecasters comprisesAcademics,Students, andOther; the remaining 70% comprises nonanalyst employees working for industrial or financial firms.Jame et al. (2016),Adebambo et al. (2016), andDa and Huang (2020)show that forecasts by Estimize participants, including those by novice forecasters, provide incremental information to Wall Street’s consensus and affect equity returns. We use a comprehensive sample of quarterly EPS forecasts submitted on the Estimize platform between 2013 and 2019. We begin the sample in 2013 because although the first official year of the platform is 2011, the population of forecasters is relatively sparse before 2013. These data uniquely enable us to explore the relative effect of sleep disruptions on information processing of nonprofessional forecasters compared with professional forecasters. Similar toDa and Huang (2020), we exclude forecasts that Estimize flags as unreliable and earnings periods with less than 20 forecasts. We also exclude erroneous duplicate forecasts, forecasts issued after the earnings announcement dates, forecasters with missing professional background information, and forecasts with horizons greater than 200 days. Our focus on forecasts with horizons less than 200 days ensures that we do not examine forecasts issued during vastly different portions of the earnings period. Last, we exclude forecasts issued during weekend days because we expect there are differences between when novice and professional forecasters produce these forecasts: professional forecasters likely produce them during the week and submit them during the weekend, whereas novice forecasters more likely produce these forecasts on the weekend outside their normal business hours.7Table 1describes our sample of Estimize forecasts and forecasters. Table 1.Summary Statistics (Estimize) Table 1.Summary Statistics (Estimize) Notes.This table presents summary statistics for our sample of EPS forecasts from Estimize. Panel A reports summary statistics for forecasts and forecasters for our full sample and for our event window covering the 60 calendar days surrounding spring DST Sundays. Panel B reports summary statistics for forecasts issued onDST days(i.e., the Monday and Tuesday following spring DST clock shifts) and forecasts issued during the two months surrounding these DST days (excluding the DST days and weekends). Panel C reports summary statistics for forecasts issued by professional and nonprofessional forecasters in the full sample. Panel D reports summary statistics for the forecasts issued by professional and nonprofessional forecasters in the event window sample. The sample of forecasts in each panel is restricted to forecasts between 2013 and 2019 that (a) are not flagged by Estimize as unreliable, (b) are issued for earnings periods that receive at least 20 forecasts, (c) are issued no later than the earnings announcement date, and (d) have horizons of no more than 200 days. All variables are defined in the appendix. The first four columns ofTable 1, panel A, report summary statistics for the sample of 443,299 forecasts in Estimize that satisfy these sample restrictions. The average forecaster has been submitting forecasts to the platform for almost two years and covering the average firm for roughly one year. Additionally, the average forecaster issues forecasts about 38 days before the earnings announcement and submits forecasts that are off by approximately 14.1 cents, or 33 basis points as a fraction of the firm’s lagged stock price. The median forecaster issues forecasts for 385 different firms, and approximately 56% of the forecasts are submitted by nonprofessionals.
article.4. Empirical Analysis: 4.1. Empirical Design To identify the effects of sleep disruptions on individual forecaster forecast accuracy, we examine variation in forecast accuracy around spring DST clock changes. Specifically, we collect the Sunday start dates of daylight saving time for each year from 2013 to 2019.8We classify forecasts issued during the Monday and the Tuesday immediately following DST Sunday as the treatment sample, that is, the days during which forecasters are most likely to be exposed to sleep disruptions. We focus on this two-day period ending three days after the clock shift because we exclude weekend forecasts, and prior research suggests that one hour of sleep loss takes between three and four subsequent days to fully recover from (Harrison 2013,Kitamura et al. 2016). To ensure local identification and to minimize the effect of confounding information events, we limit our focus to forecasts issued during the 30 calendar days on each side of the spring DST treatment window. These steps reduce our regression sample to 55,925 quarterly forecasts (1,534 issued during the spring DST treatment window and 54,391 issued during the surrounding days), issued for 1,312 firms, and by 2,269 analysts. The right four columns of panel A ofTable 1show that the average forecast accuracy and horizon are similar between our event window sample and the broader universe of forecasts (i.e., all nonweekend calendar days). The average forecast accuracy for the two groups is −0.41 and −0.33, respectively, and the average horizon is 38.3 days and 36.5 days. The percentage of forecasts issued by nonprofessional forecasters is also similar in both periods (55% relative to 56%). Importantly, we conduct our entire analysis on the sample of forecasts issued during the two months surrounding the two-day spring DST treatment window. Thus, our identifying assumptions do not require that forecast behavior is similar between our event window and the full sample but rather only that there are no shifts in forecasting behavior, other than those caused by sleep disruptions, during the days following the clock shift relative to the other days during the 62-calendar-day event window. Although our inclusion of forecaster fixed effects in our regressions makes it unlikely that forecaster selection effects contribute to our main estimator, it can nonetheless be useful to understand how forecasts issued immediately after spring DST differ from forecasts in the surrounding two months, as well as howprofessional forecasters compare with nonprofessional forecasters. Panel B ofTable 1shows that forecasts issued during DST treatment days tend to be less accurate (according to price-scaled accuracy), have slightly shorter horizons, follow a higher number of prior forecasts (but similar forecast dispersion), and be more often issued by nonprofessional forecasters compared with forecasts issued during non-DST days in our event window. Forecasts immediately following spring DST also tend to be issued by forecasters covering fewer firms and covering firms for a similar amount of time, and these forecasters have similar past accuracy compared with forecasters issuing during the surrounding two months. The fact that forecasts issued immediately after spring DST have slightly shorter horizons and are issued after a greater number of peer forecasts compared with forecasts issued during the surrounding two months helps alleviate concerns that more difficult forecasts are concentrated during our post-DST Monday/Tuesday treatment period. Panels C and D ofTable 1compare professional and novice forecasters in the full sample and in our regression sample (i.e., event window), respectively. The panels show that novice forecasters make up a larger fraction of both the full sample and the regression sample than professional forecasters. These nonprofessional forecasters have slightly higher unconditional accuracy compared with professional forecasters, but nonprofessionals tend to issue their forecasts closer to earnings announcements (30 days versus 49 days in the full sample) and after more peer analysts have issued their forecasts (90 prior forecasts versus 60 prior forecasts in the full sample). These differences in average forecast horizon and clustering are notable because, as we show in Table IA3 in the internet appendix, when we control for forecast horizon and the number and dispersion of recent peer forecasts, the unconditionally higher accuracy for nonprofessional forecasters compared with professional forecasters mentioned above becomes a conditionally lower accuracy for nonprofessional forecasters. This result supports the intuition that professional forecasters are on average better at forecasting earnings than novices. We begin our empirical analysis by estimating ordinary least squares (OLS) regressions that compare forecast accuracy during days following spring DST with forecast accuracy during surrounding days. Specifically, we estimate the following regression equation: Xijftrepresents controls for the horizon of the forecast (Forecast Horizon), which has been shown to negatively relate to forecast accuracy (see, e.g.,Ivkovic and Jegadeesh 2004); the number of forecasts submitted by other forecasters before forecasteri’s forecast, whichDa and Huang (2020)show affects forecasteri’s forecast behavior; the volatility of forecasts submitted before forecasteri’s forecast, because greater dispersion implies a less useful consensus; the number of firms that forecastericovers during yeart; the number of months that forecasterihas been covering firmj; and the average accuracy of forecasteri’s forecasts during the previous year. Our primary dependent variable,Price-Scaled Accuracy, is (the negative of) forecasteri’s absolute forecast error,−1×|𝐹𝑜𝑟𝑒𝑐𝑎𝑠𝑡𝑒𝑑𝐸𝑃𝑆𝑖𝑗𝑓𝑡−𝐴𝑐𝑡𝑢𝑎𝑙𝐸𝑃𝑆𝑗⁢𝑓|−1×|ForecastedEPSijft−ActualEPSj⁢f|, scaled by firmj’s stock price as of 100 days prior to the forecast. To ensure that our results are not driven by the scalar, we also examine unscaled accuracy, computed as (the negative of) forecasteri’s absolute forecast error (Unscaled Accuracy). To reduce the influence of outliers, we winsorize each dependent variable at the top and bottom 1% tails; results are similar when we winsorize at the top and bottom 2%, 2.5%, and 5% tails.Daylight Saving Time, our treatment variable, is an indicator variable equal to one for forecasts issued on either the Monday or Tuesday following spring DST Sunday. If the assumptions of the model hold, the effect of DST-related sleep disruptions on forecaster accuracy is estimated through the coefficient onDaylight Saving Time,β1. We predict that𝛽1<0β1<0. To test our main hypothesis discussed inSection 2—that sleep disruptions more negatively affect the information processing quality of nonprofessional forecasters compared with professional forecasters—we estimate regressions similar toEquation (1), but interact our mainDaylight Saving Timetreatment variable with a measure of forecaster expertise using forecasters’ professional-background information from Estimize. Specifically, we estimate the following model: 4.2. Main Results Table 2presents results from regressions estimated usingEquation (1). Columns (1) and (2) are estimated usingPrice-Scaled Accuracyas the dependent variable; columns (3) and (4) are estimated usingUnscaled Accuracyas the dependent variable. Columns (1) and (3) present results without control variables; columns (2) and (4) present results with the full set of control variables. Across the four columns, although all of the coefficients are negative, we find no significant effect of spring DST on average forecast accuracy. These results suggest that sleep disruptions, on average, produce no impact on the information processing quality of a broad sample of both novice and professional forecasters. This finding is consistent with prior evidence on the effects of sleep disruptions on aggregate market outcomes, which more often find that equilibrium market-level outcomes are similar following spring DST compared with other periods. This finding is also generally consistent with research in the health and medical fields finding no effect of spring DST on aggregate hospital admissions (Jin and Ziebarth 2020) and mixed evidence on the effect of spring DST on aggregate heart attacks (Kirchberger et al. 2015). Table 2.Forecast Accuracy—Average Effect (Estimize) Table 2.Forecast Accuracy—Average Effect (Estimize) Notes.This table reports results from OLS regressions estimating the relation between spring DST clock shifts and analyst forecast accuracy. The dependent variable in columns (1) and (2),Price-Scaled Accuracy, is (the negative of) analysti’s absolute forecast error, computed as−1×|𝐹𝑜𝑟𝑒𝑐𝑎𝑠𝑡𝑒𝑑𝐸𝑃𝑆−𝐴𝑐𝑡𝑢𝑎𝑙𝐸𝑃𝑆|×100−1×|ForecastedEPS−ActualEPS|×100, scaled by the firm’s share price 100 calendar days prior to the forecast. The dependent variable in columns (3) and (4),Unscaled Accuracy, is (the negative of) analysti’s unscaled absolute forecast error.Daylight Saving Timeis an indicator variable equal to one for forecasts issued during the two-day period beginning on the Monday following spring DST Sunday. The sample of forecasts in each regression is restricted to forecasts between 2013 and 2019 issued during the two-day DST treatment window or the 30 calendar days on each side of the treatment window. The sample is further restricted to forecasts (i) not flagged by Estimize as unreliable, (ii) issued for earnings periods that receive at least 20 forecasts, (iii) issued no later than the earnings announcement date, and (iv) with horizons less than 200 days. Variables are defined in the appendix. Standard errors are two-way clustered at the analyst and firm levels. FE, fixed effect; Adj., adjusted. *, **, and *** reflect significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively. InTable 3, we examine whether novice forecasters respond differently to sleep disruptions compared with professional forecasters. We useEquation (2)to estimate these regressions and employ the same dependent variables asTable 2.10Across the four columns ofTable 3, we find consistent evidence that our difference-in-differences estimate,β1, is significantly negative, indicating that the gap in forecast accuracy between professional and nonprofessional forecasters widens following spring DST clock changes. Column (1) shows the gap in forecast accuracy between nonprofessionals and professionals widens by 7.5 basis points during the Monday and Tuesday period following spring DST, which represents an 18.3% reduction relative to the average forecast accuracy of −41 basis points. This relative reduction is almost identical after we add controls in column (2), indicating that our estimated effect of DST is not likely influenced by differences in the types of analysts forecasting during our treatment and control periods or differences in forecast characteristics across these two periods for either professional or nonprofessional forecasters.11Columns (3) and (4) show that the DST effect remains significant when we useUnscaled Accuracyas the dependent variable, indicating our estimated DST effect is not driven by our price scalar.12 Table 3.Forecast Accuracy—Conditional on Expertise (Estimize) Table 3.Forecast Accuracy—Conditional on Expertise (Estimize) Notes.This table presents results from OLS regressions estimating the relation between spring DST clock shifts and the relative forecast accuracy of professional and nonprofessional forecasters. The dependent variable in columns (1) and (2),Price-Scaled Accuracy, is (the negative of) analysti’s absolute forecast error, computed as−1×|𝐹𝑜𝑟𝑒𝑐𝑎𝑠𝑡𝑒𝑑𝐸𝑃𝑆−𝐴𝑐𝑡𝑢𝑎𝑙𝐸𝑃𝑆|×100−1×|ForecastedEPS−ActualEPS|×100, scaled by the firm’s share price 100 calendar days prior to the forecast. The dependent variable in columns (3) and (4),Unscaled Accuracy, is (the negative of) analysti’s unscaled absolute forecast error.Daylight Saving Timeis an indicator variable equal to one for forecasts issued during the two-day period beginning on the Monday following spring DST Sunday.Nonprofessionalis an indicator variable equal to one when the forecaster’s professional background in Estimize is listed as “Non Professional.” Although suppressed to conserve space, each control variable reported is also interacted withNonprofessional. The sample of forecasts in each regression is restricted to forecasts between 2013 and 2019 issued during the two-day DST treatment window or the 30 calendar days on each side of the treatment window. The sample is further restricted to forecasts (i) not flagged by Estimize as unreliable, (ii) issued for earnings periods that receive at least 20 forecasts, (iii) issued no later than the earnings announcement date, and (iv) with horizons less than 200 days. Variables are defined in the appendix. Standard errors are two-way clustered at the analyst and firm levels. *, **, and *** reflect significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively. To address the concern that our control period of 30 calendar days on each side of the treatment window may be too large, in untabulated analyses, we reestimate our regressions after trimming the estimation window from 30 calendar days on each side to 21 calendar days or 14 calendar days on each side. Although doing so reduces our sample size by 38% and 62%, respectively, we continue to find a negative effect of spring DST on the relative forecast accuracy of nonprofessional forecasters, withp-values below 0.10, when using these more localized samples. This result suggests it is unlikely that our results are driven by the control period being too temporally distant from the treatment period. To address a related concern that a reduction in management guidance forecasts during our post-spring-DST treatment period may drive the relative reduction in forecast accuracy rather than sleep disruptions, we also confirm in untabulated analyses that (i) managers do not appear to systemically reduce their likelihood of issuing guidance forecasts during our treatment window and (ii) controlling in our regressions for whether management issues a guidance forecast the same day the forecaster issues their forecast does not change our results.13 4.3. Parallel Trends Analysis and Placebo Tests 4.3.1. Parallel Trends. We next explore the possibility that our results are due to preexisting trends in the relative forecast accuracy of nonprofessional forecasters leading up to spring DST clock shifts. For such patterns to drive our results, there needs to be a downward trend in the relative forecast accuracy of nonprofessional forecasters preceding the clock shift such that the relative forecast accuracy of this group would be expected to underperform the accuracy of professional forecasters even without the spring DST shock. To explore this possibility, we estimate a parallel trends analysis inFigure 1by plotting the conditional difference between nonprofessional forecaster accuracy and professional forecaster accuracy over various two-day windows surrounding spring DST. Specifically, we augment the regression specification in column (2) ofTable 3by interacting theNonprofessionalindicator variable with a series of indicators for two-day event windows surrounding spring DST. We estimate these regressions using our full 30-calendar-day sample period on each side of the spring DST treatment window, and then we include indicators for 11 different two-day event windows covering the period [−13, +16] relative to spring DST Sunday, excluding weekend days. Thus, each interaction coefficient is benchmarked to the average relative forecast accuracy during the two event windows [−29, −13] and [+17, +31]. The results inFigure 1indicate two findings. First, there is no trend in the relation between ourNonprofessionalindicator and the event windows preceding spring DST, suggesting that any estimated effect immediately following spring DST is not the result of a broader pattern in the relation between professional status and relative forecast accuracy. Second, the coefficient reflecting the period immediately following spring DST—marked “0” on thex-axis and representing the two-day period beginning on the Monday following spring DST Sunday—indicates a significant reduction in the relative forecast accuracy of nonprofessional forecasters during this two-day period, and this coefficient is the only significantly negative coefficient in the figure. This result indicates there is a unique drop in the relative forecast accuracy of novice forecasters during the period immediately following spring DST. 4.3.2. Placebo Tests. We next explore the possibility that our results are due to a spurious correlation between spring DST clock shifts and the relative forecast accuracy of nonprofessional forecasters. In Table IA6 of the internet appendix, we estimate placebo regressions similar to those inTable 3, but we alter the treatment window from the two-day period beginning on the Monday following spring DST to the two-day period beginning on the Monday immediately preceding this treatment Monday (i.e., Monday/Tuesday during the week leading up to spring DST). We include forecasts during the 60 calendar days surrounding this placebo DST treatment window and estimate regressions using our two measures of forecast accuracy. We find no evidence of a change in relative forecast accuracy of nonprofessional forecasters compared with professional forecasters during this period. The results inFigure 1and Table IA6 are consistent with the notion that the relative ability of nonprofessional forecasters to process financial information declines following spring DST because of sleep disruptions that disproportionately negatively affect the cognition of individuals with less domain-specific expertise. Notably, our evidence is the first that sleep disruptions negatively affect the information processing quality of individual market participants. However, we show that the negative effect of sleep disruptions on the information processing ability of market participants is concentrated in participants with less domain-specific expertise, which is consistent with prior evidence that analysts with more professional work experience are better able to make use of a given set of resources (Bradley et al. 2017) and further alleviates concerns that our results are spurious. Intuitively, forecasters with more expertise are better able to mitigate the influence of transitory sleep disruptions. 4.4. Fall DST Changes In Table IA7 in the internet appendix, we examine analysts’ forecast accuracy following fall DST changes, when most individuals in the United States are exposed to an extra hour in their days. Prior evidence finds little change in sleep, health, or performance outcomes around fall DST (e.g.,Barnes and Wagner 2009,Smith 2016). Specifically,Barnes and Wagner (2009, p. 1305)find following fall DST that “there are no significant differences in sleep, injury quantity, or injury severity” (abstract). As a result, we predict no relative decline in accuracy for forecasters with less expertise following fall DST. Consistent with our prediction, Table IA7 shows no change in forecast accuracy during the two-day period beginning on the Monday following fall DST Sundays, when looking at either the aggregate average forecast accuracy (untabulated) or the relative forecast accuracy of professional and nonprofessional forecasters.14The fact that we find a significant relative decline in accuracy for novice forecasters following spring DST, an event that prior research overwhelmingly concludes disrupts individuals’ sleep, but not following fall DST, an event that prior research generally concludes does not affect individuals’ sleep, supports the notion that sleep disruptions are the driver of the relative reduction in forecast accuracy following spring DST for novice forecasters. 4.5. I/B/E/S Sample 4.5.1. Average Effect. In this section, we perform quasi-out-of-sample tests of the effect of sleep disruptions on the information processing of financial analysts using Refinitiv’s I/B/E/S database, which includes exclusively forecasts submitted by professional brokerages. We employ a sample from I/B/E/S not to examine a separate hypothesis from the ones examined above but to understand whether our documented effects generalize to a sample of professional brokerages and to further rule out the possibility that our Estimize-sample results are spurious. Prior research documents many differences in the timing and composition of forecasts submitted to I/B/E/S compared with forecasts submitted to Estimize (Jame et al. 2016), making I/B/E/S forecasts a useful sample for validation. We structure our I/B/E/S-sample analysis as closely to our Estimize-sample analysis as possible. In particular, we begin by again estimatingEquation (1), using a sample of quarterly EPS forecasts between 2013 and 2019 issued during the two months surrounding spring DST, with horizons of 200 days or less and earnings periods with at least 20 forecasts. We also rely on the same dependent variables and include the same control variables in our regressions. Given our results inTable 2, we hypothesize thatβ1is statistically indistinguishable from zero. We report the results from this analysis inTable 4. The sample is further restricted to forecasts (i) issued for earnings periods that receive at least 20 forecasts, (ii) issued no later than the earnings announcement date, and (iii) with horizons less than 200 days. Across all four columns ofTable 4, we find thatβ1is not significantly different from zero. These results on aggregate forecast accuracy are consistent with our primary evidence on aggregate forecast accuracy in our Estimize-sample analysis. The results again support the notion that forecast accuracy of professional analysts is,on average, relatively immune to spring DST clock shifts shown to negatively affect individual decision makers in other contexts. 4.5.2. Relative Effect of Analyst Expertise. Because I/B/E/S only reports forecasts for professional sell-side analysts, when using these data we face limitations in testing our main hypothesis that sleep disruptions disproportionately affect the forecast accuracy of forecasters with less expertise. However, wecanexamine whether sleep disruptions disproportionately affect the forecast accuracy of sell-side analysts with less expertise relative to other sell-side analysts. InTable 5, we reestimateEquation (2)using our two main measures of forecast accuracy and replace theNonprofessionalindicator with an indicator,Low Expertise, which denotes sell-side analysts who are likely to have relatively low expertise.Low Expertiseis equal to one when the analyst’s forecasting experience is in the bottom quartile of the annual distribution, where forecasting experience is the number of months for which the analyst has been submitting forecasts to I/B/E/S since 1983.15We continue to control for the horizon of the forecast, the number of forecasts that have been submitted by other analysts before analysti’s forecast, the dispersion of forecasts that have been submitted before analysti’s forecast, the number of firms that analysticovers during yeart, the number of months that analystihas been covering firmj, and the average accuracy of analysti’s forecasts during the previous year, along with the interactions of these control variables withLow Expertise. For the sample in column (2), forecasts by low-expertise analysts make up 24.4% (or 61,578) of the 251,905 total forecasts and 26.9% (or 2,052) of the 7,624 forecasts issued during the treatment period immediately following spring DST. We predict that𝛽1<0β1<0. Across the four columns ofTable 5, we find an economically and statistically significant relative decline in forecast accuracy following spring DST for sell-side analysts with lower expertise. Given an average scaled forecast accuracy of −2.02, the results in columns (1)–(2) indicate a relative reduction of between 10.9% and 11.4% for low-expertise analysts following spring DST, respectively. To ensure that these results are not driven by differences in the coverage of firms or earnings periods between I/B/E/S and Estimize, in Table IA9, we reestimateTable 5after restricting the sample to the set of firm-earnings periods included in our Estimize sample. We find similar results. The results inTable 5and Table IA9 provide more generalized evidence that sleep disruptions disproportionately affect the information processing quality of market participants with less domain-specific expertise. With the caveats mentioned above in mind regarding measuring forecaster experience in the Estimize sample, in Table IA10, we perform an analysis similar toTable 5using our Estimize sample to examine whether forecaster experience moderates the effect of sleep disruptions among the sample of Estimize forecasters. In panel A, we pool professional and nonprofessional forecasters and interactDaylight Saving Timewith an indicator for whether the forecaster is in the bottom quartile of experience that year (Low Expertise). In panel B, we interact our mainDaylight Saving Time×Nonprofessionalindependent variable withLow Expertise. In panel A, we find marginally significant evidence in columns (3) and (4) that the effect of sleep disruptions on forecaster accuracy is more pronounced among forecasters with less experience. In panel B, we find consistently significant evidence across all four columns that the relative decline in forecast accuracy for novice forecasters following spring DST is more pronounced for novices with less experience.16These results suggest that even with the noted drawbacks to measuring experience in this sample, we find generally consistent evidence across our I/B/E/S-sample and Estimize-sample analyses that experience, in particular, influences how participants manage sleep disruptions. 4.5.3. Moderating Effect of Analyst Resources. Our results in the previous section show that the impact of sleep disruptions on analysts’ information processing varies across multiple dimensions of analysts’ expertise—for example, novice versus professional, experienced versus inexperienced, and level of experience conditional on professional status. These findings motivate an additional prediction based on analysts’ access to resources. As we discuss inSection 2, because there exists wide variation across analysts in the level of resources they can lean on when their own information processing quality suffers, we expect that the quality of an analyst’s resources interacts with their level of expertise. More specifically, we predict that low access to resources amplifies the negative effect of low expertise. We exploit the unique granularity of the I/B/E/S data to test this prediction. Because analysts’ resources tend to be increasing in the reputation of the analysts’ brokerages, we proxy for an analyst’s resources using the reputation of their employer (i.e., brokerage size). We then reestimate the regressions inTable 5and interact our mainDaylight Saving Time×Low Expertiseindependent variable with an indicator variable,Low Resources, which equals one when the analyst’s brokerage is in the bottom quartile of size that year, measured using the number of analysts employed by the brokerage. We continue to include the same control variables as inTable 5, and we include all interaction combinations betweenDaylight Saving Time,Low Expertise, andLow Resources, but we suppress these coefficients again for brevity. We report the results of this analysis inTable 6. Across the four columns ofTable 6, we find consistent evidence that the negative effect of low expertise on how analysts respond to sleep disruptions is concentrated among analysts with low resources. For example, column (1) suggests that theDaylight Saving Time×Low Expertisedifference-in-differences estimate is more than an order of magnitude larger in absolute value when resources are low compared with when resources are high. These results have potential implications for brokerages in that they suggest institutions with better access to information, human capital, and tangible resources can potentially alleviate the impact of their analysts’ cognitive constraints. 4.6. Confounding Effects of Weather and International Evidence 4.6.1. Weather. One disadvantage of using spring DST clock shifts to identify the effect of sleep disruptions is that the clock shifts occur during the same part of each year. Further, this part of the year, toward the end of winter, tends to have colder and more unpleasant weather than on average, and prior research shows that unpleasant weather negatively affects how analysts process information and forecast earnings (Dehaan et al. 2017). Importantly, because our regressions compare analysts’ forecasting during the two days following spring DST to surrounding days, some of which likely have more pleasant weather and some of which likely have less pleasant weather, our research design largely neutralizes the effects of weather on our main coefficient estimates. However, because I/B/E/S discloses the employer of each analyst, we are able to use our I/B/E/S sample to more directly control for the effects of weather. We begin by collecting from BrokerCheck and internet searches the geographic location of each analyst at the time of their forecast.17We then collect from Visual Crossing daily weather information for each city in which the analysts are located. We then augmentEquation (2)by including controls for the daily average temperature and cloud cover, along with interactions of these variables with ourLow Expertiseindicator variable. The results, presented in Table IA12 in the internet appendix, show that we continue to find an economically and statistically significant relative decline in forecast accuracy for low-expertise analysts following spring DST. This evidence suggests that our results are not likely driven by uniquely unpleasant weather affecting analysts during our post-DST treatment period. 4.6.2. International Evidence. Although, as we mention above, one downside of using DST clock shifts to study the effect of sleep disruptions in the United States is that the shifts occur at the same time each year, an important advantage of using DST clock shifts is that DST is implemented at different times of the year across the world (e.g., during the fall in the Southern Hemisphere, rather than during the spring) and not implemented at all in many places. As a result, we can perform additional out-of-sample tests using analysts in non-U.S. countries subject to different DST regimes to better understand whether our documented effects are due to sleep disruptions, or simply due to confounding (non-sleep-related) effects that happen to coincide with two specific days during the third week of March each year. To implement these analyses, we use the I/B/E/S international detail file to identify non-U.S. countries that have different DST dates than the United States and a sufficiently large sample of forecasts to reestimate our analyses. Brazil, a Southern Hemisphere country that shifts its clocks forward during the fall season in the United States and has a well-developed analyst industry, is the only country to meet these criteria. We then collect DST beginning dates for Brazil and estimate regressions similar to those inTable 5but center our regression sample on Brazil’s DST beginning dates (we substitute analyst fixed effects with brokerage fixed effects because of our limited sample of forecasts for each analyst). We expect to find a relative decline in the forecast accuracy of analysts with low expertise during the two days following Brazil’s clock shift, even though the clock shifts occur during the fall in the United States. In addition to the Brazilian sample, we also search for testable samples among East Asian countries, which do not adjust their clocks to daylight saving time during our sample period. Of these countries, South Korea and Japan represent the two most heavily populated samples in I/B/E/S. We then reestimate our analysis inTable 5, but separately use samples of forecasts issued by South Korean and Japanese brokerages. Because we utilize in this analysis U.S. spring DST dates that affect sleep patterns in the United States but do not affect sleep patterns in South Korea and Japan, we predict null results for the effects of the post-DST treatment period in these two East Asian subsamples. We are unable to observe market trading prices for a large portion of the companies covered by analysts in these countries, so we use unscaled forecast accuracy as our main dependent variable; to account for more extreme outliers in these samples, we winsorize this dependent variable at the top and bottom 5% tails. Our results are qualitatively similar when we use forecast accuracy scaled by the absolute value of the average forecast error. We present the results from our Brazilian-, South Korean-, and Japanese-sample analyses in columns (1)–(2), (3)–(4), and (5)–(6) ofTable 7, respectively. The sample of forecasts in each regression is restricted to forecasts between 2013 and 2019 issued during the two-day DST treatment window or the 30 calendar days on each side of the treatment window. The sample is further restricted to forecasts (i) issued for earnings periods that receive at least 20 forecasts, (ii) issued no later than the earnings announcement date, and (iii) with horizons less than 200 days. Variables are defined in the appendix. Standard errors are two-way clustered at the analyst and firm levels. *, **, and *** reflect significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively. We find evidence consistent with our predictions for the three respective countries. Columns (1) and (2) show a significant relative decline in forecast accuracy among low-expertise analysts in Brazil following the Sunday when Brazilians shift their clocks forward one hour. In contrast, in columns (3)–(4) and (5)–(6), we find no evidence of a relative decline in forecast accuracy among low-expertise analysts when using placebo U.S. spring DST dates in the South Korean and Japanese samples, respectively. These results provide compelling evidence that the driver of the reductions in relative forecast accuracy in the United States following spring DST is sleep disruptions. One caveat to these results is that because the sample sizes are much smaller than the sample sizes in our main tests, it is possible the results from these international tests have less external validity than our main results.
article.5. Conclusion: Prior experimental and field evidence suggests sleep disruptions negatively affect individuals’ information processing abilities, but there is mixed evidence of similar effects in financial markets. We help reconcile this incongruity by focusing on the information processing abilities of individual forecasters and on how the effects of sleep disruptions vary based on individuals’ domain-specific expertise. Using crowdsourced EPS forecasts from forecasters with a wide range of professional backgrounds, we find that sleep disruptions following spring DST clock shifts do not negatively affect the forecast quality of the average analyst but significantly reduce the relative forecast accuracy of novice forecasters compared with professional forecasters. Consistent with our main results, we also find when using a separate sample of forecasts from I/B/E/S that spring DST reduces the relative forecast accuracy of professional sell-side analysts with less domain-specific expertise. We offer several pieces of evidence that support the conclusion that sleep disruptions are the primary driver of the relative decline in forecast accuracy for forecasters with less expertise following spring DST: (i) we find no evidence of trends in the relative forecast accuracy of novice forecasters before or after spring DST; (ii) we find no change in the relative forecast performance of novices around fall DST, when individuals typically do not experience a change in their sleep patterns; (iii) we show that seasonal weather patterns do not affect our results; (iv) we observe no relative decline in forecast accuracy during a placebo period a week prior to spring DST or among analysts in countries that do not change their clocks in the spring; and (v) we document a significant relative decline in accuracy during a different portion of the year among a sample of forecasters who lose an hour of sleep during the fall season in the United States. We also provide evidence that there are several dimensions along which expertise moderates the effect of sleep disruptions on analyst information processing, including experience in the industry and employer resources. Our paper is the first to provide evidence of how sleep disruptions affect the information processing ofindividualfinancial market participants. Our results are consistent with sleep disruptions negatively affecting cognition and information processing ability, but only among individuals with less domain-specific expertise to lean on when they are not functioning at the peak of their mental abilities. Our findings are relevant to recent discussions among financial service providers regarding burnout among junior banking analysts. Specifically, our results indicate that junior analysts are more susceptible to the negative consequences of sleep disruptions than their more experienced colleagues. Finally, our results speak to the current debate among policymakers regarding the continuation of daylight saving time. We show that sleep disruptions associated with daylight saving time clock shifts negatively affect people’s abilities to process financial information and make forecasts about the future. Overall, whereas prior studies largely conclude financial market participants are immune from the effects of sleep disruptions, we provide evidence of specific examples when sleep disruptions meaningfully affect participants.
